hints.accusative: Accusative of 'vis' would be 'vim' — 'vis' here is nominative singular subject
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'nulla vi' — 'Nulla vis' is nominative
hints.genitive: Genitive of 'vis' is rare in singular — 'vis' here is nominative

hints.3rd declension (regular): 'Vis' is 3rd declension but highly irregular — it lacks genitive/dative singular forms
hints.4th declension: 'Vis' is not 4th declension — it is an irregular 3rd declension noun

hints.never: 'Numquam' means 'never' — 'umquam' means 'ever,' and 'nulla' provides the negation

hints.to come together: 'To come together' is 'convenire' — 'convellet' comes from 'convellere' (to tear away)
hints.to wish: 'To wish' is 'velle' — 'convellere' means to tear/wrench away
hints.to carry: 'To carry' is 'vehere' — 'convellere' means to tear away

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'convellit' — 'convellet' with -et is future indicative (3rd conj.)
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'convellat' — 'convellet' is future indicative
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'convulsit' or 'convelli' — 'convellet' is future

hints.volo, velle, volui, —: These are the parts of 'velle' (to wish) — an irregular verb, not related to 'convellere'
hints.veho, vehere, vexi, vectum: These are the parts of 'vehere' (to carry) — different verb
hints.vello, vellere, velli, vulsum: These are the parts of 'vellere' (to pluck) — the base verb, but 'convellere' is the compound

steps.prompt: What does the preposition 'a' govern here?

hints.accusative: 'A/ab' always takes the ablative, not the accusative
hints.genitive: 'A/ab' governs the ablative — genitive is not used with 'a'
hints.dative: 'A/ab' governs the ablative — dative is not used with 'a'

steps.reveal: meis manibus = from my hands (abl. pl.)

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'meae manus' — 'meis manibus' is ablative
hints.accusative: Accusative plural would be 'meas manus' — 'meis manibus' is ablative governed by 'a'
hints.dative: 'Manibus' could be dative plural, but here 'a' requires ablative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'manus' (gen.) ends in -ūs
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'manus' (gen.) ends in -ūs
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'manus' (gen.) ends in -ūs

hints.double negative creating a positive: In Latin, 'nulla' + 'umquam' is not a double negative — 'umquam' means 'ever,' not 'never'
hints.litotes (understatement): Litotes would be 'not without force' — this is direct emphatic denial
hints.rhetorical question: This is a declarative statement, not a question

translation: No force will ever tear (them) from my hands
